title: Working-Capital Asymmetry

content.statement: The strongest businesses often get paid before they have to pay.
content.question: Can you restructure your business pricing or payout timing so customers effectively fund your growth?
content.equation: WCA = Prepayment + Supplier Float + Deferred Delivery - Funding Need
sop: Calculate Prepayment, cash collected from customers before you deliver (see calculation, page 6).
Calculate Supplier Float, how much time and cash flexibility your suppliers give you before payment is due (see calculation, page 6).
Calculate Deferred Delivery value, revenue collected for delivery that happens later (see calculation, page 6).
Subtract your Funding Need, capital required to cover the gap between spending and collecting (see calculation, page 6).
If the result is negative, test one pricing change (deposit, annual plan, prepay discount) that shifts cash collection earlier.
content.explainer: Amazon and Costco built empires on this. Get paid first, pay later, structurally.
questions: Can you restructure your business pricing or payout timing so customers effectively fund your growth?
Do you collect payment before or after you deliver the product or service, and what would change if you flipped it?
If you doubled down on annual prepayment or deposits, how much upfront cash would that put in your account this quarter?

definitions.def: The cash a business needs to operate day-to-day.
Money you get to hold and use in the gap between collecting cash and having to pay it out. Supplier float is the breathing room a supplier gives you before payment is due.

content.equation_example: Prepayment $5,000, Supplier Float $3,000, Deferred Delivery $2,000, Funding Need $4,000. $5,000 plus $3,000 plus $2,000 minus $4,000 equals $6,000. Positive and higher means customers and suppliers are effectively funding your growth. Negative means you're financing the gap yourself, out of your own cash.
content.closing_statement: Getting paid first is a structural advantage, not luck.

caption:
Principle Nº 56: Working-Capital Asymmetry

The strongest businesses often get paid before they have to pay. Amazon and Costco built empires on this.

Ask yourself: Can you restructure your business pricing or payout timing so customers effectively fund your growth?
